children.value:
This document proves that based on free-particle Hamiltonian, if we add an attractive potential, the symmetry of
the ground state will be broken. The new ground state will be a bound state.

children.html: pip_ipi​
iii
mmm

children.value: is the mass of the particle.
The ground state is of course not free electrons. It is an interesting coherent state where particle number is not definite.
We can not create two electrons with the same spin and momentum so the exponential term is truncated to its 1st order.
Given different Cooper pair wave functions

children.html: ∣ϕk⟩| \phi_k \rangle∣ϕk​⟩

children.value: , we can construct different kinds of ground states. We notice that this ground state is not normalized.

children.value: as long as we keep the order of items with the same momentum.
It’s convenient to define creation and annihilation of Cooper pairs as follows:
We can rewrite the ground state as follows:

children.value: So Cooper pair is a boson.
We go back to the normalization of the ground state:

children.html: ⟨ψBCS⟩ψBCS=∏k⟨0∣1+ϕk⟩bk∗⟨1+ϕkbk†⟩0=∏k⟨0∣1+ϕkbk∗+ϕkbk†+ϕkϕk∗bkbk†∣0⟩=∏k⟨0∣1+ϕk⟩ϕk∗(1−bk†bk)∣0⟩=∏k(1+ϕk2)\begin{align} \braket{\psi_{BCS}}{\psi_{BCS}} & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi^*_{k \rangle b_{k}} \braket{1 + \phi_{k} b_{k}^\dagger}{0} \\ & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi^*_{k b_{k}} + \phi_{k b_{k}^\dagger} + \phi_{k} \phi^*_{k} b_{k} b_{k}^\dagger | 0 \rangle \\ & = \prod_{k} \langle 0 | 1 + \phi_{k \rangle \phi^*_{k} (1 - b_{k^\dagger b_{k}})} | 0 \rangle \\ & = \prod_{k} (1 + \phi^2_{k}) \end{align}⟨ψBCS​⟩ψBCS​​=k∏​⟨0∣1+ϕk⟩bk​∗​⟨1+ϕk​bk†​⟩0=k∏​⟨0∣1+ϕkbk​∗​+ϕkbk†​​+ϕk​ϕk∗​bk​bk†​∣0⟩=k∏​⟨0∣1+ϕk⟩ϕk∗​(1−bk†bk​​)​∣0⟩=k∏​(1+ϕk2​)​​

children.value: Based on that we can get the expectation value of Cooper pair creation/annihilation operator:

\begin{align}
    \langle \psi_{BCS |} b_{k}^\dagger | \psi_{BCS \rangle} & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) b_{k}^\dagger \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left(1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left( 1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \left( 1 + \phi_{k b^\dagger_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \langle 0 | \left(\phi^*_{k} \right) \left( 1 -  b^\dagger_{k  b_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\
    & = \phi^*_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^2_{k'}\right)  \\
\end{align}
children.html: ⟨ψBCS∣bk†∣ψBCS⟩=⟨0∣∏k′(1+ϕk′∗bk′)bk†∏k′′(1+ϕk′′bk′′†)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(1+ϕk∗bk)bk†∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′∗bk′)∏k′′(1+ϕk′′bk′′†)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(1+ϕk∗bk)bk†(1+ϕkbk†)∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′∗bk′)(1+ϕk′bk′†)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(ϕk∗)(1−bkbk†)∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′∗bk′)(1+ϕk′bk′†)∣0⟩=ϕk∗∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′2)\begin{align} \langle \psi_{BCS |} b_{k}^\dagger | \psi_{BCS \rangle} & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) b_{k}^\dagger \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\ & = \langle 0 | \left(1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\ & = \langle 0 | \left( 1 + \phi^*_{k} b_{k} \right) b^\dagger_{k} \left( 1 + \phi_{k b^\dagger_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\ & = \langle 0 | \left(\phi^*_{k} \right) \left( 1 - b^\dagger_{k b_{k}} \right) \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \left(1 + \phi_{k'} b_{k'}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\ & = \phi^*_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^2_{k'}\right) \\ \end{align}⟨ψBCS∣​bk†​∣ψBCS⟩​​=⟨0∣k′∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)bk†​k′′∏​(1+ϕk′′​bk′′†​)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(1+ϕk∗​bk​)bk†​k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)k′′∏​(1+ϕk′′​bk′′†​)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(1+ϕk∗​bk​)bk†​(1+ϕkbk†​​)k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)(1+ϕk′​bk′†​)∣0⟩=⟨0∣(ϕk∗​)(1−bkbk​†​)k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)(1+ϕk′​bk′†​)∣0⟩=ϕk∗​k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′2​)​​

⟨ψBCS∣bk∣ψBCS⟩=⟨0∣∏k′(1+ϕk′∗bk′)bk∏k′′(1+ϕk′′bk′′†)∣0⟩=⟨0∣∏k′(1+ϕk′∗bk′)∏k′′≠k(1+ϕk′′bk′′†)bk(1+ϕkbk†)∣0⟩=⟨0∣∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′∗bk′)∏k′′≠k(1+ϕk′′bk′′†)(1+ϕkbk∗)ϕk(1−bk†bk)∣0⟩=ϕk∏k′≠k(1+ϕk′2)\begin{align} \langle \psi_{BCS |} b_{k} | \psi_{BCS \rangle} & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) b_{k} \prod_{k''} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) | 0 \rangle \\ & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''\neq k} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) b_{k} \left(1 + \phi_{k} b_{k}^\dagger\right)| 0 \rangle \\ & = \langle 0 | \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^*_{k'} b_{k'}\right) \prod_{k''\neq k} \left(1 + \phi_{k''} b_{k''}^\dagger\right) \left(1 + \phi^*_{k b_{k}}\right) \phi_{k} \left( 1 - b_{k^\dagger b_{k}}\right)| 0 \rangle \\ & = \phi_{k} \prod_{k'\neq k} \left(1 + \phi^2_{k'}\right) \\ \end{align}⟨ψBCS∣​bk​∣ψBCS⟩​​=⟨0∣k′∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)bk​k′′∏​(1+ϕk′′​bk′′†​)∣0⟩=⟨0∣k′∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)k′′=k∏​(1+ϕk′′​bk′′†​)bk​(1+ϕk​bk†​)∣0⟩=⟨0∣k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′∗​bk′​)k′′=k∏​(1+ϕk′′​bk′′†​)(1+ϕkbk​∗​)ϕk​(1−bk†bk​​)∣0⟩=ϕk​k′=k∏​(1+ϕk′2​)​​

children.value: It might give us a hint that the average of field operator over a coherent state is the wave function.
And we can get a mean-field theory by averaging the 4-point interaction term over Cooper channel.

children.html: δ(bk′†)δ(bk)=(bk′†−⟨bk′⟩†)(bk−⟨bk⟩)=bk′†bk−bk′†⟨bk⟩−⟨bk′⟩†bk+⟨bk′⟩†⟨bk⟩≈0\begin{align} \delta(b^\dagger_{k'})\delta(b_{k}) & = (b^\dagger_{k'} - \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle})(b_{k} - \langle b_{k \rangle}) \\ & = b^\dagger_{k'} b_{k} - b^\dagger_{k'} \langle b_{k \rangle} - \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} b_{k} + \langle b^\dagger_{k' \rangle} \langle b_{k \rangle} \\ & \approx 0 \end{align}δ(bk′†​)δ(bk​)​=(bk′†​−⟨bk′⟩†​)(bk​−⟨bk⟩​)=bk′†​bk​−bk′†​⟨bk⟩​−⟨bk′⟩†​bk​+⟨bk′⟩†​⟨bk⟩​≈0​​

children.value: ), we can get the mean-field Hamiltonian:

children.html: H=∑k,σεkckσ†ckσ+Vk,k′e-phck↑†ck′↑†c−k′↓c−k↑→MF∑k,σεkckσ†ckσ+∑kΔkck↑†c−k↓†+Δ−k∗c−k↓ck↑+Δ−k∗Δk→Nambu Spinor∑k,σΨk†(h⃗k⋅τ⃗)Ψk+Δ−k∗Δk\begin{align} H = & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + V^{\text{e-ph}}_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} c^\dagger_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} c^\dagger_{\mathbf{k'}\uparrow} c_{-\mathbf{k'}\downarrow} c_{-\mathbf{k}\uparrow} \\ \xrightarrow{\text{MF}} & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow}^\dagger + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} \\ \xrightarrow{\text{Nambu Spinor}} & \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} (\vec{h}_{\mathbf{k}} \cdot \vec{\tau}) \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \Delta^*_{-\mathbf{k}} \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} \\ \end{align}H=MF​Nambu Spinor​​k,σ∑​εk​ckσ†​ckσ​+Vk,k′e-ph​ck↑†​ck′↑†​c−k′↓​c−k↑​k,σ∑​εk​ckσ†​ckσ​+k∑​Δk​ck↑†​c−k↓†​+Δ−k∗​c−k↓​ck↑​+Δ−k∗​Δk​k,σ∑​Ψk†​(hk​⋅τ)Ψk​+Δ−k∗​Δk​​​

children.value: Now we have the BCS Hamiltonian. We might get the partition function by path integral of the Fermion field for the four-point interaction.
Using Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, we can trade the four-point interaction of the fermion field for an effective bosonic field coupling with a 2-point fermion field.
Firstly, the path integral of the fermion field is:

Z = \int \mathcal{D} \bar{c^\dagger} \mathcal{D} c \exp 
\left( \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} V_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k'}\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} c_{\mathbf{k'}\uparrow} \right)
If we don’t do a mean-field but directly expand the exponential function with respect to the 4-point interaction term. We will get a series:

children.html: Z=∫Dc†ˉDc(∑k,k′Vk,k′ck↑†c−k′↓†c−k↓ck′↑)nn!exp⁡(∑k,σεkckσ†ckσ)\begin{align} Z = \int \mathcal{D} \bar{c^\dagger} \mathcal{D} c \frac{\left(\sum_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} V_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k'}} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k'}\downarrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} c_{\mathbf{k'}\uparrow}\right)^n}{n!} \exp \left( \sum_{\mathbf{k}, \sigma} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} \right) \end{align}Z=∫Dc†ˉDcn!(∑k,k′​Vk,k′​ck↑†​c−k′↓†​c−k↓​ck′↑​)n​exp⎝⎛​k,σ∑​εk​ckσ†​ckσ​⎠⎞​​​

children.value: Using the functional derivative we convert the functional average into a derivative of Gaussian integral with analytical expression.

children.html: δδjkσ†ˉ∫Dc†ˉDcexp⁡(∑kεkckσ†ckσ+∑kjkσckσ+∑kjkσ†ckσ†)=δδjkσ†ˉexp(∑kεk−1jkσ†jkσ)\begin{align} \frac{\delta}{\delta \bar{j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger}} \int \mathcal{D} \bar{c^\dagger} \mathcal{D} c \exp \left( \sum_{\mathbf{k}} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{k}} j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{k}} j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger \right) = \frac{\delta}{\delta \bar{j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger}} exp \left( \sum_{\mathbf{k}} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}^-1} j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger j_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} \right) \end{align}δjkσ†​ˉ​δ​∫Dc†ˉDcexp(k∑​εk​ckσ†​ckσ​+k∑​jkσ​ckσ​+k∑​jkσ†​ckσ†​)=δjkσ†​ˉ​δ​exp(k∑​εk−1​jkσ†​jkσ​)​​

children.value: From this kind of unperturbed Hamiltonian and linear coupling we can’t get finite average of Cooper pair amplitude.

But if we write the coupling in the quadratic form, we can get a finite average of Cooper pair amplitude.
$$

children.html: δδΔkexp⁡(∑kεkckσ†ckσ+Δk†ck↑†c−k↓†+Δkck↑c−k↓)\begin{align} \frac{\delta}{\delta \Delta_{\mathbf{k}}} \exp \left( \sum_{\mathbf{k}} \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \Delta_{\mathbf{k}}^\dagger c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^\dagger c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow}^\dagger + \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} \right) \end{align}δΔk​δ​exp(k∑​εk​ckσ†​ckσ​+Δk†​ck↑†​c−k↓†​+Δk​ck↑​c−k↓​)​​

children.value: Now that we have a Hamiltonian of quadratic form, we can write it in a matrix form.
If we define Nambu spinors and write this Hamiltonian on this basis, it would be easy to diagonalize it and get the eigen pairs.

children.html: Ψk†=(ck↑†c−k↓),Ψk=(ck↑c−k↓†)\begin{align} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} = \begin{pmatrix} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow}^{\dagger} & c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow} \end{pmatrix}, \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} = \begin{pmatrix} c_{\mathbf{k}\uparrow} \\ c_{-\mathbf{k}\downarrow}^\dagger \end{pmatrix} \end{align}Ψk†​=(ck↑†​​c−k↓​​),Ψk​=(ck↑​c−k↓†​​)​​

children.value: Diagonalizing the Hamiltonian will give us the eigen states (the supreconducting state) and the ground state energy of the system.
Gap function is obtained by minimizing the free energy.

children.value: It is a bit un-intuitive to understand the meaning of the Nambu spinors.

For normal spinors like Dirac spinors, it’s a 4 component vector where different
entry represents the wavefunction of particle anti-particle with spin up/down.

For Nambu spinors, it’s a 2 component vector where different
entry represents the wavefunction of particle with spin up/down.

But Nambu spinor is a vector of operators. Straightforwardly, we might think well it can
generate a 2-component wavefunction upon operatoring on the vacuum state.
Anyway, we might write the Hamiltonian as a 2

children.html: ×\times×

children.value: We do it step by step, firstly we write the creation and annihilation operators in the Nambu basis.

children.html: ck†c−k†=Ψk†(0100)Ψk′c−kck=Ψk†(0010)Ψk′\begin{align} c_{k}^\dagger c_{-k}^\dagger & = \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \Psi_{\mathbf{k'}} \\ c_{ -k} c_{k} & = \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \Psi_{\mathbf{k'}} \\ \end{align}ck†​c−k†​c−k​ck​​=Ψk†​(00​10​)Ψk′​=Ψk†​(01​00​)Ψk′​​​

children.value: We all know that 2-dimensional traceless unitary matrix is a vector space.
There basis are the very well-known Pauli matrices. We cite it here for convenience.

children.html: σx/σ1=(0110),σy/σ2=(0−ii0),σz/σ3=(100−1)\begin{align} \sigma_x / \sigma_1= \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \sigma_y / \sigma_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -i \\ i & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \sigma_z / \sigma_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \end{align}σx​/σ1​=(01​10​),σy​/σ2​=(0i​−i0​),σz​/σ3​=(10​0−1​)​​

children.value: We can define creation and annihilation operators based on the Pauli matrices.

children.html: σ+=12(σ1+iσ2)=(0100)σ−=12(σ1−iσ2)=(0010)\begin{align} \sigma_+ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \sigma_1 + i \sigma_2 \right) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \\ \sigma_- = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \sigma_1 - i \sigma_2 \right) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \\ \end{align}σ+​=2​1​(σ1​+iσ2​)=(00​10​)σ−​=2​1​(σ1​−iσ2​)=(01​00​)​​

children.value: So the creation and annihilation operators can be written as:

children.html: ck†c−k†=Ψk†σ+Ψkc−kck=Ψk†σ−Ψk\begin{align} c_{k}^\dagger c_{-k}^\dagger & = \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \sigma_+ \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} \\ c_{ -k} c_{k} & = \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \sigma_- \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} \\ \end{align}ck†​c−k†​c−k​ck​​=Ψk†​σ+​Ψk​=Ψk†​σ−​Ψk​​​

children.value: We can then write the BCS ground state as:

children.html: ∣ψBCS⟩=∏k(1+ϕkσ+)∣0⟩\begin{align} | \psi_{BCS \rangle} = \prod_{k} \left(1 + \phi_{k} \sigma_+\right) | 0 \rangle \\ \end{align}∣ψBCS⟩​=k∏​(1+ϕk​σ+​)∣0⟩​​

children.value: We can write the Hamiltonian in the Nambu basis as:

children.html: H=∑kεkΨk†Ψk+∑kΔkΨk†σ+Ψk+Δ−k∗Ψk†σ−Ψk+Δ−k∗Δk=∑kΨk†(εkσ0+Δkσ++Δ−k∗σ−)Ψk+Δ−k∗Δk\begin{align} H & = \sum_{k} \varepsilon_{k} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \sum_{k} \Delta_{k} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \sigma_+ \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \Delta^*_{-k} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \sigma_- \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \Delta^*_{-k} \Delta_{k} \\ & = \sum_{k} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} (\varepsilon_{k} \sigma_0 + \Delta_{k} \sigma_+ + \Delta^*_{-k} \sigma_- ) \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \Delta^*_{-k} \Delta_{k} \\ \end{align}H​=k∑​εk​Ψk†​Ψk​+k∑​Δk​Ψk†​σ+​Ψk​+Δ−k∗​Ψk†​σ−​Ψk​+Δ−k∗​Δk​=k∑​Ψk†​(εk​σ0​+Δk​σ+​+Δ−k∗​σ−​)Ψk​+Δ−k∗​Δk​​​

children.value: It will be more convenient to write it in Pauli matrix basis instead of creation/annihilation operators.
But refactoring it won’t be a big job. Just separate the real and imaginary parts.

children.html: H=∑kΨk†(εkσ0+Re(Δk)σ1+Im(Δk)σ2)Ψk+Re(Δk∗)Im(Δk)\begin{align} H & = \sum_{k} \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} (\varepsilon_{k} \sigma_0 + \mathrm{Re}(\Delta_{k}) \sigma_1 + \mathrm{Im}(\Delta_{k}) \sigma_2 ) \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} + \mathrm{Re}(\Delta^*_{k}) \mathrm{Im}(\Delta_{k}) \\ \end{align}H​=k∑​Ψk†​(εk​σ0​+Re(Δk​)σ1​+Im(Δk​)σ2​)Ψk​+Re(Δk∗​)Im(Δk​)​​

children.value: But we will firstly make some comments on the Hamiltonian.
Firstly, if there is no gap, the Hamiltonian is just the free-particle Hamiltonian.
However we can still evaluate the Hamiltonian on coherent state.
We know that for normal spinors, we usually make

σ3k=Ψk†σ3Ψk=(ck†ck−c−kc−k†)=nk+n−k−1\begin{align} \sigma_{3k} & = \Psi_{\mathbf{k}}^{\dagger} \sigma_3 \Psi_{\mathbf{k}} \\ & = (c_{k}^\dagger c_{k} - c_{-k} c_{-k}^\dagger) \\ & = n_k + n_{-k} - 1 \\ \end{align}σ3k​​=Ψk†​σ3​Ψk​=(ck†​ck​−c−k​c−k†​)=nk​+n−k​−1​​

children.value: We notice that ground state and Cooper pair states are both eigenstates of

±1\pm 1±1

children.value: We find this correspondance between spinors and Nambu spinors.
These two eigen vectors spans the mixture of ground state and Cooper pair states.

children.value: , we can always find a unitary transformation to diagonalize the Hamiltonian.
One may solve the Dyson equation of the Nambu-Gor’kov equation to get the interacting Green’s function,

children.html: G^−1(k,ω)=G0^−1(k,ω)−Σ^(k,ω)=G0^−1(k,ω)−τ3^G0^τ3^D(k,ω)\begin{align} \hat{G}^{-1}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) & = \hat{G_0}^{-1}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) - \hat{\Sigma}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) \\ & = \hat{G_0}^{-1}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) - \hat{\tau_3} \hat{G_0} \hat{\tau_3} D(\mathbf{k}, \omega) \\ \end{align}G^−1(k,ω)​=G0​^​−1(k,ω)−Σ^(k,ω)=G0​^​−1(k,ω)−τ3​^​G0​^​τ3​^​D(k,ω)​​

children.value: Symmetry breaking of the MF Hamiltonian

children.label: Symmetry breaking of the MF Hamiltonian

children.value: We will see here how the

children.html: U(1)U(1)U(1)

children.value: symmetry is broken by the mean-field Hamiltonian and how it leads to a breaking of conservation of particle number.
We will see that it preserve the

children.html: SU(2)SU(2)SU(2)

children.value: Spin-fluctuation can serve as a mediation of attractive potential between electrons, where phonon plays this role in conventional superconductors.
But here we want to see the interplay between spin-fluctuation and phonon mediation.
The best case is that spin-fluctuation is also kind of a quadratic form.
Or maybe it’s a quatic interactino but we take the mean-field approximation.
